title: Refund-Risk Architecture

content.question: What's your business's expected refund rate, and which of your offers is structurally most refund-prone?
content.equation: RRA = (Expectation Gap + Impulse + Time to Value + Friction) ÷ 4
sop: List your offers ranked by current or estimated refund rate.
Rate your highest-refund offer 1-10 on Expectation Gap, how far the marketing promise sits from the actual first experience (see calculation, page 5).
Rate it 1-10 on Impulse, how quickly people buy without deliberation (see calculation, page 5).
Rate it 1-10 on Time to Value, how long before the customer feels the benefit (see calculation, page 5).
Rate it 1-10 on Friction to get support or clarity after purchase (see calculation, page 5), then fix whichever factor scores highest before your next launch.
content.explainer: Refunds aren't random. They're a function of offer structure. Design them out before launch.
questions: What's your business's expected refund rate, and which of your offers is structurally most refund-prone?
Which of your offers gets bought fastest and returned most, and have you ever connected those two facts?
If a customer waited two weeks to get real value from your product, would that gap be enough to trigger a refund request?
content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10: Expectation Gap 7, Impulse 8, Time to Value 6, Friction 5. RRA = (7 + 8 + 6 + 5) ÷ 4 = 6.5. Higher marks offers structurally built for refund spikes — fix the marketing promise or speed up time-to-value; lower means the offer sets expectations customers actually experience, so refunds stay low.
content.closing_statement: Refund rates are designed in, not random accidents.

caption:
Principle Nº 69: Refund-Risk Architecture

Certain offers structurally attract refunds. Refunds aren't random.

Ask yourself: What's your business's expected refund rate, and which of your offers is structurally most refund-prone?
